step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("nitrous oxide anesthesia limitations")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

Based on the detailed information gathered regarding nitrous oxide usage limitations, there remains strong support for why it isn't utilized solely as a general anesthetic (GA) agent. The key point reiterated across various sources is the inherent inadequacy of nitrous oxide in terms of delivering sustained high enough oxygen concentrations necessary throughout prolonged surgical procedures - making it impractical if not unsafe to rely exclusively on just N<sub>2</sub>O for GA purposes. Additional corroborating details highlight issues like slower induction rates compared to some alternatives plus limited utility in certain specialized fields requiring precise control over neural suppression depth & quality. Therefore, I reaffirm my agreement with Option **\boxed{A}** stating difficulty in maintaining adequate oxygen concentration stands firm as principal barrier hindering independent employment of nitrous oxide as exclusive GA medium. No new compelling counterevidence emerged challenging established consensus view presented initially here.
Based on the comprehensive review of the evidence, we see continued emphasis on the fundamental challenge faced by relying solely on nitrous oxide for general anesthesia—the persistent concern surrounding its capability to maintain adequate oxygen concentrations continuously over longer durations typical of many surgical procedures. This aligns perfectly with the initial reasoning provided. Furthermore, several pieces of literature reinforce this notion by discussing practical difficulties encountered even when combining nitrous oxide with other agents to enhance its efficacy and reliability.
Given this robust confirmation and lack of any substantial contradictory evidence introduced by recent findings, I remain convinced that the primary limitation stems from the necessity to ensure continuous and reliable oxygen supply, which nitrous oxide alone fails to meet adequately. Hence, my final stance solidifies my agreement with the chosen option:

Based on the detailed information gathered regarding nitrous oxide usage limitations, there remains strong support for why it isn't utilized solely as a general anesthetic (GA) agent. The key point reiterated across various sources is the inherent inadequacy of nitrous oxide in terms of delivering sustained high enough oxygen concentrations necessary throughout prolonged surgical procedures - making it impractical if not unsafe to rely exclusively on just N<sub>2</sub>O for GA purposes. Additional corroborating details highlight issues like slower induction rates compared to some alternatives plus limited utility in certain specialized fields requiring precise control over neural suppression depth & quality. Therefore, I reaffirm my agreement with Option **\boxed{A}** stating difficulty in maintaining adequate oxygen concentration stands firm as principal barrier hindering independent employment of nitrous oxide as exclusive GA medium. No new compelling counterevidence emerged challenging established consensus view presented initially here.
Based on the comprehensive review of the evidence, we see continued emphasis on the fundamental challenge faced by relying solely on nitrous oxide for general anesthesia—the persistent concern surrounding its capability to maintain adequate oxygen concentrations continuously over longer durations typical of many surgical procedures. This aligns perfectly with the initial reasoning provided. Furthermore, several pieces of literature reinforce this notion by discussing practical difficulties encountered even when combining nitrous oxide with other agents to enhance its efficacy and reliability.
